name: Big-O Notation — The Language of Efficiency

metadata.career_relevance: Every Flipkart, Amazon, Zerodha, and Razorpay interview starts with Big-O. Your solution works? Great. Is it O(n²) when O(n log n) exists? You won't clear the round. Zerodha's trading engine processes 10 million orders per day — an O(n²) sort would take 10¹⁴ operations vs O(n log n)'s 2.3×10⁸. That's the difference between a sub-second response and one that takes days. After this topic, you'll think about efficiency before writing a single line of code.

hook.real_world_problem:
Zerodha order matching without Big-O thinking:

```
Naive approach: For each incoming order, scan ALL existing orders
Code: for each new order → loop through 10M orders to find match
Complexity: O(n²) — 10M × 10M = 10¹⁴ operations
Time: Would take ~115 days for one trading session

With Big-O thinking:
Use a sorted order book (binary search + heap)
Complexity: O(log n) per match — log₂(10M) ≈ 23 operations
Time: Microseconds per match
Result: NSE processes 1M+ orders/second with no lag

Big-O is why Zerodha can handle 10 crore trades on Diwali
without crashing. It's the most important concept in DSA.
```
hook.what_you_will_build: The mental model to classify any algorithm's efficiency — and communicate it in technical interviews.

concept_explanation.big_o_definition:
**Big-O: describes how runtime grows as input grows**

```
Big-O answers: 'If I double the input size, what happens to the time?'

O(1)       — Constant:     doesn't matter (array index, HashMap get)
O(log n)   — Logarithmic:  halves the problem each step (binary search)
O(n)       — Linear:       one pass through data (single loop)
O(n log n) — Linearithmic: sort the data (merge sort, TimSort)
O(n²)      — Quadratic:    nested loops (bubble sort, naive search)
O(2ⁿ)      — Exponential:  try every combination (brute-force recursion)

Growth rate visualized (n = 1,000,000):
  O(1)       → 1 operation
  O(log n)   → ~20 operations
  O(n)       → 1,000,000 operations
  O(n log n) → ~20,000,000 operations
  O(n²)      → 1,000,000,000,000 operations (1 trillion) ← unusable
```

visual_aid.description: Big-O growth rate graph. X-axis: input size n (0 to 100). Y-axis: operations (0 to 10,000). Six curves: O(1) flat at bottom, O(log n) barely rising, O(n) diagonal, O(n log n) slightly above diagonal, O(n²) steep parabola, O(2ⁿ) rockets off the chart. Each curve labeled with color and typical example: O(1)=HashMap.get(), O(log n)=Binary Search, O(n)=Linear Search, O(n log n)=Merge Sort, O(n²)=Bubble Sort, O(2ⁿ)=All Subsets. Key annotation: 'At n=100, O(n²)=10,000 ops vs O(n log n)=664 ops'
visual_aid.alt_text: Big-O complexity growth curves showing dramatic difference between O(n log n) and O(n²)

worked_example.scenario: Classify the Big-O of 5 Zerodha order-processing code snippets.
worked_example.problem_statement: Without running any code, determine the Big-O time complexity of each snippet. Justify with one sentence.

step_by_step_solution.details: Each loop that iterates once over n elements = O(n). Two sequential loops = O(2n) = O(n) (drop constants). The key: does the loop run n times? If yes, it's O(n) at minimum.
Outer loop runs n times, inner loop runs n times for EACH outer iteration: n × n = n². BUT: if inner loop doesn't depend on n (e.g., always runs 10 times), it's still O(n).
If each step cuts the remaining work in half: binary search on sorted array, dividing by 2 each time. How many times can you halve n before reaching 1? log₂(n) times.

worked_example.expected_output:
n=1,000,000 orders:
  O(1):       1 operation
  O(log n):   20 operations
  O(n):       1,000,000 operations
  O(n log n): 20,000,000 operations
  O(n²):      1,000,000,000,000 operations ← 50,000x slower than sort
things_to_notice: Constants don't matter at scale: O(100n) and O(n) are both 'O(n)'. A loop that does 100 things per iteration is still linear. What matters is HOW the time grows with n, not the absolute time.
The dominant term wins: if your algorithm is O(n² + n), the n² term completely overwhelms n for large inputs. At n=1000: n²=1,000,000 vs n=1,000. The n is 0.1% of the total — we ignore it.
log n is almost constant: log₂(1,000,000,000) = ~30. Even for a billion elements, binary search takes only 30 steps. This is why sorted data + binary search is so powerful.
O(n log n) is the practical sorting limit: you can't sort faster than O(n log n) in the general case (comparison-based sort). Any interview answer that claims O(n) sort for arbitrary data is wrong.

industry_examples.use_case: 100 million products. User searches 'iPhone 15 blue 128GB'. O(n) linear scan = 100M comparisons per search = 10 seconds. O(log n) indexed search (inverted index + binary search) = ~30 comparisons = milliseconds. Big-O difference between a working product and an unusable one.

applications.interview_relevance: Every technical interview at FAANG India, Zerodha, Razorpay, Flipkart: after you code a solution, the interviewer asks 'What's the time and space complexity?' If you can't answer in 10 seconds, it signals you're not thinking about efficiency. The expected format: 'This is O(n log n) time and O(n) space because...' — one sentence justification.
applications.real_world_workflow: Algorithm analysis workflow: (1) identify the input size variable(s) — usually n, (2) find the most expensive operation (usually the innermost loop or recursion), (3) count how many times it runs in terms of n, (4) drop constants and non-dominant terms, (5) state the Big-O class with justification.

common_gaps.what_students_get_wrong: Thinking a loop that always runs exactly 100 times is O(n). It's O(1) — because 100 is a constant, not n.
common_gaps.remediation: Ask: 'Does the number of iterations grow with input size n?' If no → O(1). If the loop runs `array.length` times → O(n). If it runs always exactly 10 or 100 times → O(1).
common_gaps.detection_pattern: Student labels `for (int i = 0; i < 10; i++) {}` as O(n) instead of O(1)

key_takeaways: Big-O describes growth rate, not absolute time — O(n) means linear growth
O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(n²) < O(2ⁿ) — memorize this order
Drop constants: O(3n) = O(n). Drop non-dominant: O(n² + n) = O(n²)
Nested loops: multiply. Sequential loops: add (then drop non-dominant)
Different inputs: use different variables — O(a + b), not O(n)
log n growth is magical: log₂(1 billion) ≈ 30 steps
O(n log n) is the practical sorting lower bound
Always state Big-O with justification in interviews

self_check_questions.question: Zerodha has 5 million active traders. Algorithm A processes each trader in O(log n) time. Algorithm B is O(n). How many times faster is A than B?
self_check_questions.answer: n = 5,000,000. O(n) = 5,000,000 operations. O(log n) = log₂(5,000,000) ≈ 22.25 operations. A is 5,000,000 ÷ 22 ≈ 227,000 times faster. This is why binary search on a sorted trader list vs linear scan is the difference between microseconds and seconds. At Zerodha's scale, choosing the wrong algorithm would make the system unusable.

reflection.next_topic_preview: Next: Analyzing Code Complexity — given any block of Java code, systematically calculate its Big-O time complexity step by step.
